past_medical_history: HIV infection diagnosed 3 years ago, on antiretroviral therapy for 2 months
Hospitalized 2 months ago for headache and confusion, treated empirically for cryptococcal and bacterial meningitis at outside hospital, discharged on fluconazole prophylaxis
CD4 count 2 months ago was 359 cells/mm3
History of recurrent oral aphthous ulcers
No known history of syphilis or other sexually transmitted infections

medications.indication: Antifungal prophylaxis following empiric treatment for cryptococcal meningitis

family_history: Mother deceased at age 55 from unknown cause
Father age 68 with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Two siblings alive and reportedly healthy
No known family history of neurological disease or autoimmune conditions

social_history.occupation: Long-distance truck driver, currently on medical leave
social_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol use, approximately 2-3 beers per week
social_history.tobacco: Smokes 5-10 cigarettes per day for the past 15 years
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current use; remote history of marijuana use in his twenties
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife and three children in a peri-urban area
social_history.sexual_history: Married, reports prior unprotected sexual encounters with multiple partners before marriage; wife is HIV-negative on most recent testing
social_history.travel: Travels frequently across sub-Saharan Africa for work

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but appears confused and mildly agitated. GCS 15 (E4 V5 M6). Oriented to person and place but initially gives incorrect date, self-corrects. Speech is fluent but tangential at times with occasional inappropriate laughter. Attention impaired: unable to spell WORLD backwards, manages only 3 serial 7s before losing track. Recall 2/3 objects at 5 minutes, recalls third with phonemic cue. Appears disinhibited, making inappropriate comments during examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally to light touch and pinprick. Facial movements symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone normal in upper and lower extremities. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested bilaterally (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius). No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No sensory level. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with normal base and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Nuchal rigidity present with resistance to passive neck flexion. Kernig sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski sign positive. No rash, petechiae, or purpura on full skin examination. An ulcerated lesion approximately 1.5 cm in diameter noted on the right buccal mucosa near the molar region, present for approximately 3 weeks per patient report. A firm, mildly tender left submandibular lymph node approximately 2 cm in diameter is palpated. Mild left-sided jaw swelling noted, patient attributes to biting his cheek during a recent episode of teeth clenching at night.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent headache, confusion, restlessness, and left-sided chest pain for 1 day, previously hospitalized 2 months ago for meningitis
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Okonkwo is a 42-year-old right-handed man with known HIV infection diagnosed three years ago who presents to the emergency department with a one-day history of worsening headache, confusion, restlessness, and left-sided chest pain. He was hospitalized two months prior at an outside facility for a similar presentation of headache and altered mental status, where he was treated empirically with intravenous ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial meningitis and high-dose fluconazole for possible cryptococcal meningitis. At that time, he was also initiated on antiretroviral therapy with tenofovir, lamivudine, and efavirenz, along with co-trimoxazole prophylaxis. His CD4 count at the prior admission was 359 cells per cubic millimeter. He reports partial improvement after the initial hospitalization and was discharged with instructions to continue his antiretroviral regimen and fluconazole prophylaxis.

Over the past two weeks, his wife has noticed progressive changes in his behavior, including increased irritability, occasional episodes of inappropriate laughter, and difficulty with everyday tasks that he previously performed without difficulty. He has been sleeping poorly and appears restless at night. Three days ago, he developed a dull, diffuse headache that has gradually worsened. Yesterday, he became noticeably confused, unable to recall recent conversations, and more agitated than usual. His wife brought him to the emergency department today after he complained of sharp left-sided chest pain and appeared increasingly disoriented.

The patient reports that he has had a persistent ulcer on the inside of his right cheek near the molar area for approximately three weeks. He has not sought dental care for this lesion and has been managing it with saltwater rinses. He also notes intermittent mild left jaw swelling that he attributes to bruxism during sleep. He denies any recent fever, though his wife recalls that he felt warm to the touch during his prior hospitalization. He denies cough, shortness of breath at rest, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, rash, or joint pains. He has been adherent to his antiretroviral medications and co-trimoxazole but admits he occasionally forgets the fluconazole.

His prior meningitis workup at the outside hospital was reportedly negative for cryptococcal antigen and tuberculosis, though detailed records are not available. He has no history of seizures, but his wife describes two brief episodes over the past week where he appeared to stare blankly and his arms stiffened for approximately 30 seconds before returning to baseline, which she attributed to fatigue. He has not had any witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizures. He has not traveled outside his usual routes in the past six months and has no known sick contacts with tuberculosis.

findings.location: Right frontal lobe, subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense, ill-defined lesion in right frontal subcortical white matter
signal_characteristics.DWI: No definite restricted diffusion within the lesion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No enhancement of the lesion itself

findings.mass_effect: Mild local sulcal effacement, no midline shift

findings.location: Bilateral frontoparietal and temporal convexities, more prominent on the right

signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Subtle, patchy leptomeningeal enhancement along bilateral frontoparietal convexities with mild enhancement extending into the Sylvian fissures bilaterally. Right-sided predominance.
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity in the right frontoparietal region

signal_characteristics.T2: Scattered punctate hyperintensities in periventricular white matter bilaterally

additional_observations: Ventricles and sulci are within normal limits for age
No acute hemorrhage or midline shift
No subdural collection or epidural empyema identified
Normal vascular flow voids in major intracranial vessels
Scattered periventricular white matter changes may be related to HIV-associated small vessel disease or other etiology
mri.impression: Ill-defined nonenhancing subcortical lesion in the right frontal lobe with subtle surrounding edema, differential includes HIV-associated white matter disease, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), low-grade glioma, or an atypical infectious process. Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement is noted along bilateral frontoparietal convexities, which may represent an active meningeal process or post-infectious changes. Scattered periventricular white matter changes consistent with small vessel disease, possibly HIV-related. Clinical and CSF correlation strongly recommended.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Possible, though lack of restricted diffusion is atypical for acute PML

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Active meningeal infectious process (bacterial, fungal, or tuberculous)

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Should be considered given HIV status and clinical context

recommended_actions: Urgent CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, cultures, and special stains
CSF VDRL and FTA-ABS testing given HIV status
JC virus PCR on CSF to exclude PML
Consider repeat imaging in 2-4 weeks to assess for interval change

findings: No evidence of acute ischemia or pericarditis
ecg.interpretation: Normal ECG. No evidence of acute coronary syndrome or pericarditis to explain the left-sided chest pain.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Left-sided chest pain is likely musculoskeletal or pleuritic in nature rather than cardiac. Clinical correlation advised.

CBC.clinical_significance: Leukopenia, consistent with HIV-related immunosuppression or bone marrow effect of co-trimoxazole
Mild neutropenia; may blunt the inflammatory response to bacterial infection
Mild anemia, could be related to HIV, chronic disease, or ART effects
Low, consistent with anemia of chronic disease

Inflammatory_markers.clinical_significance: Moderately elevated, suggests an inflammatory or infectious process but lower than expected for fulminant bacterial meningitis
Elevated, nonspecific indicator of inflammation; could reflect HIV, chronic infection, or active meningitis
Mildly elevated; in the gray zone (0.25-0.50 ng/mL), does not clearly distinguish bacterial from non-bacterial etiology

LFTs.clinical_significance: Upper normal limit, may reflect efavirenz hepatic effects
Mildly elevated, could be ART-related or hepatic congestion
Mild hypoalbuminemia, may reflect chronic illness or poor nutritional status

HIV_parameters.clinical_significance: Moderate immunosuppression; susceptible to opportunistic infections but above threshold for many AIDS-defining illnesses
Detectable viral load, ART only started 2 months ago; expected to take 3-6 months to achieve viral suppression

labs.interpretation: Leukopenia with mild neutropenia and lymphopenia consistent with HIV infection and possible co-trimoxazole effect. Mild anemia of chronic disease. Inflammatory markers moderately elevated but not in the range typically seen with fulminant bacterial meningitis (CRP 28.5, procalcitonin 0.35 in gray zone). Mildly elevated AST and low albumin may reflect ART effects and chronic illness. CD4 count 312 cells/mm3 with detectable viral load on recently initiated ART. Normal renal function and electrolytes. These findings are nonspecific and could be consistent with a partially treated or indolent infectious meningitis, chronic inflammatory process, or early immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).

csf.interpretation: Clear CSF with elevated opening pressure and markedly elevated protein (204.7 mg/dL) but near-normal cell count (3 WBC/uL) and near-normal glucose. This albuminocytologic dissociation pattern is atypical for acute bacterial meningitis and raises a broad differential including partially treated bacterial meningitis, early or indolent infection, neurosyphilis, tuberculous meningitis, carcinomatous meningitis, or Guillain-Barre syndrome. Cryptococcal and TB causes have been excluded by current testing. Gram stain and cultures are negative, which may reflect prior antibiotic therapy. Further specialized testing recommended including CSF VDRL, cytology, and meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel.

output.glucose: 28 mg/dL (low, reference 40-70 mg/dL; serum glucose 102 mg/dL)

special_tests.Gram_stain: Rare gram-positive diplococci seen on concentrated specimen
special_tests.Culture: Streptococcus pneumoniae isolated at 72 hours (penicillin MIC 0.06 ug/mL - susceptible)
special_tests.Meningitis_PCR_panel: Streptococcus pneumoniae detected; all other targets negative

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture now reveals frank bacterial meningitis with neutrophilic pleocytosis (85 WBC/uL, 72% neutrophils), markedly elevated protein (312.4 mg/dL), low glucose (28 mg/dL, ratio 0.27), elevated lactate (6.2 mmol/L), and Streptococcus pneumoniae identified on Gram stain, culture, latex agglutination, and PCR panel. CSF VDRL is nonreactive, arguing against neurosyphilis. This represents a partially treated or slowly evolving pneumococcal meningitis, with the initial LP likely performed too early in the course or partially suppressed by prior empiric antibiotic therapy. Immediate high-dose IV antibiotics with dexamethasone are indicated.

Blood_cultures.clinical_significance: Bacteremia with S. pneumoniae supports diagnosis of pneumococcal meningitis with hematogenous spread
Concordant positive culture in second set confirms true bacteremia

Blood_cultures.value: Penicillin MIC 0.06 (S), Ceftriaxone MIC <0.06 (S), Vancomycin MIC 0.5 (S), Meropenem MIC <0.06 (S)
Blood_cultures.clinical_significance: Fully susceptible organism; ceftriaxone monotherapy appropriate once susceptibilities confirmed

output.interpretation: Two out of two aerobic blood culture sets positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae, confirming pneumococcal bacteremia. The organism is fully susceptible to penicillin, ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and meropenem. This finding supports the diagnosis of pneumococcal meningitis with secondary bacteremia and indicates hematogenous dissemination.
abnormal_values_summary: Blood cultures positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae (2/2 sets)

output.interpretation: CSF meningitis/encephalitis multiplex PCR panel is positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae only. All viral, other bacterial, and fungal targets are negative. This confirms pneumococcal meningitis and argues against viral encephalitis, cryptococcal meningitis, and other bacterial pathogens.
abnormal_values_summary: Streptococcus pneumoniae DETECTED on CSF meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel

output.interpretation: Syphilis serologic testing is entirely negative. RPR nonreactive, FTA-ABS nonreactive, and CSF VDRL nonreactive. Neurosyphilis is effectively excluded. The oral ulcer is likely aphthous in nature rather than syphilitic.

signal_characteristics.density: Hypodense relative to normal white matter, nonenhancing on post-contrast imaging

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Subtle meningeal enhancement bilaterally, more conspicuous than on prior imaging

output.impression: CT head without and with contrast shows a stable ill-defined hypodense lesion in the right frontal subcortical white matter and bilateral meningeal enhancement that appears slightly more prominent compared to the prior MRI description. No contraindication to lumbar puncture identified. No evidence of raised intracranial pressure with mass effect or herniation. The findings are consistent with an active meningeal process.
recommended_actions: Proceed with lumbar puncture as no contraindication identified
Consider MRI with contrast for better characterization of the right frontal lesion

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha, poorly sustained, reactive to eye opening

findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests right frontal structural or functional disturbance, correlates with the MRI lesion
Suggests mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction, consistent with encephalopathy

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient cooperation difficulty

output.impression: Abnormal EEG showing intermittent right frontotemporal focal slowing and mild diffuse background slowing consistent with encephalopathy. No definite epileptiform discharges identified during this 30-minute recording. The focal slowing correlates with the right frontal lesion seen on neuroimaging. The episodes described by the wife may warrant prolonged EEG monitoring to exclude subclinical seizures.
output.limitations: Limited cooperation, recording duration 30 minutes, no sleep captured
recommended_actions: Consider continuous EEG monitoring if seizure is suspected
Repeat EEG after treatment of underlying condition

output.query: bacterial meningitis in HIV patients partially treated atypical CSF findings

results.title: Bacterial Meningitis in HIV-Infected Patients: Atypical Presentations and Diagnostic Challenges
results.summary: HIV-infected patients with bacterial meningitis may present atypically with normal or near-normal CSF cell counts due to impaired immune response, particularly with CD4 counts below 500 cells/mm3. Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common causative organism. Elevated CSF protein with low cell count (albuminocytologic dissociation) can occur and may be mistaken for non-infectious etiologies.

results.title: Pneumococcal Meningitis in the Setting of HIV Infection: Clinical Features and Outcomes
results.journal: Journal of Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndromes
results.summary: Pneumococcal meningitis remains the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in HIV-positive adults. Initial CSF may show blunted pleocytosis in immunosuppressed patients. Mortality is higher than in HIV-negative patients. Early empiric antibiotics with ceftriaxone and vancomycin plus dexamethasone remain the standard of care.

results.title: Recurrent Meningitis in HIV: Differential Diagnosis and Approach
results.summary: Recurrent meningitis in HIV patients requires consideration of partially treated bacterial meningitis, cryptococcal meningitis, TB meningitis, neurosyphilis, and immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). A systematic approach with comprehensive CSF testing including cultures, molecular diagnostics, and serologic markers is essential.

output.summary: In HIV-infected patients, bacterial meningitis (especially pneumococcal) may present with atypical CSF findings including low or normal cell counts due to immunosuppression, markedly elevated protein, and negative initial Gram stains. The blunted immune response can mask the typical neutrophilic pleocytosis. Repeat lumbar puncture, molecular diagnostics (PCR panels), and blood cultures are critical for diagnosis. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics should not be delayed pending confirmatory testing.

output.proposed: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h + Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg IV q6h with current ART (tenofovir/lamivudine/efavirenz) and co-trimoxazole
interactions: Dexamethasone may reduce efavirenz levels by inducing CYP3A4 metabolism; consider monitoring efavirenz trough levels or using alternative corticosteroid dosing strategy
Vancomycin and tenofovir are both nephrotoxic; monitor renal function closely during concurrent use
warnings: Ensure adequate hydration to minimize nephrotoxicity risk from vancomycin plus tenofovir
Monitor for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) as patient is on recently initiated ART
Dexamethasone-efavirenz interaction may require ART regimen adjustment if prolonged steroid use is needed
output.formulary_status: All medications available on hospital formulary
alternatives: If penicillin-resistant pneumococcus, consider meropenem as alternative to ceftriaxone
If steroid interaction with efavirenz is concerning, consider dolutegravir-based ART switch

Audiometry.value: Mild sensorineural hearing loss at 4000-8000 Hz (thresholds 35-40 dB)
Audiometry.clinical_significance: Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, may be early complication of bacterial meningitis or pre-existing (efavirenz/co-trimoxazole ototoxicity less likely)

Audiometry.value: Normal hearing thresholds across all frequencies (thresholds 10-20 dB)

Audiometry.clinical_significance: Mildly reduced speech discrimination on the right, consistent with sensorineural hearing loss

output.interpretation: Asymmetric hearing loss with mild right-sided sensorineural hearing loss at high frequencies. This may represent an early complication of bacterial meningitis (cochlear inflammation) or could be pre-existing. Baseline audiometry is important for monitoring treatment-related ototoxicity. Recommend follow-up audiometry after completion of meningitis treatment.
abnormal_values_summary: Right ear: mild sensorineural hearing loss at 4000-8000 Hz
Right ear: mildly reduced speech discrimination (88%)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae in an HIV-infected patient with atypical initial CSF presentation

differential.likelihood: Moderate initial suspicion given HIV status and oral ulcer, but excluded by negative serology
differential.key_distinguishing: CSF VDRL nonreactive, serum RPR and FTA-ABS nonreactive; oral ulcer is aphthous not chancre

differential.likelihood: Moderate initial suspicion given HIV and elevated opening pressure
differential.key_distinguishing: Negative India ink, negative cryptococcal antigen, negative CSF PCR for Cryptococcus

differential.likelihood: Moderate given HIV, geographic risk, and elevated CSF protein
differential.key_distinguishing: GeneXpert negative for M. tuberculosis, AFB stain negative; TB meningitis typically shows more lymphocytic predominance and very low glucose

differential.likelihood: Low but considered given HIV and frontal white matter lesion
differential.key_distinguishing: PML typically presents with focal deficits without meningeal signs; JC virus PCR would be needed to exclude
IRIS typically occurs 2-8 weeks after ART start with paradoxical worsening; would need to identify underlying pathogen

differential.likelihood: Could explain cognitive changes but not meningeal signs
differential.key_distinguishing: Does not cause meningeal signs, elevated opening pressure, or markedly elevated CSF protein

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head to exclude mass lesion or raised ICP before lumbar puncture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontal hypodense lesion and meningeal enhancement; no contraindication to LP

optimal_actions.action: Analyze initial CSF results including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, and special tests
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Atypical CSF with elevated protein but near-normal cell count; negative Gram stain and cultures; negative cryptococcal and TB markers

optimal_actions.action: Review laboratory results including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, and HIV parameters
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukopenia, lymphopenia, moderately elevated CRP, procalcitonin in gray zone, low CD4 count

optimal_actions.action: Order blood cultures before starting empiric antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Streptococcus pneumoniae isolated from blood cultures

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) and adjunctive dexamethasone immediately, do not wait for culture results
Request CSF meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel for rapid pathogen identification
Order syphilis testing (serum RPR, FTA-ABS, and CSF VDRL) given HIV status and oral ulcer
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All syphilis serologies nonreactive, excluding neurosyphilis

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions between meningitis treatment and current ART regimen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dexamethasone-efavirenz CYP3A4 interaction; vancomycin-tenofovir nephrotoxicity risk

optimal_actions.action: Request repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Repeat LP now shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, S. pneumoniae on Gram stain and culture confirming diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on bacterial meningitis in HIV patients with atypical CSF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: HIV patients can have blunted CSF pleocytosis; pneumococcal meningitis most common in this population

critical_actions: Start empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) immediately without waiting for culture confirmation
Administer adjunctive dexamethasone before or with the first dose of antibiotics
Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Request CSF meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel given negative Gram stain in an immunocompromised host
Do not discontinue antiretroviral therapy during meningitis treatment
Recognize that blunted CSF pleocytosis in an HIV-infected patient does not exclude bacterial meningitis
contraindicated_actions: Delaying empiric antibiotics pending confirmatory CSF culture results
Dismissing bacterial meningitis based on low CSF WBC count alone in an immunocompromised patient
Performing lumbar puncture without prior CT imaging in a patient with focal neurological signs or altered mental status
Administering carbamazepine without checking interactions with efavirenz (both CYP3A4 substrates/inducers)
Discontinuing ART during acute meningitis treatment
Empiric treatment with fluconazole alone assuming recurrent cryptococcal disease without confirmatory testing
key_reasoning_points: The initial CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation (markedly elevated protein 204.7 mg/dL with only 3 WBC/uL), which is atypical for bacterial meningitis but can occur in immunocompromised patients with blunted inflammatory response
HIV-related immunosuppression (CD4 312) can mask the classic neutrophilic pleocytosis of bacterial meningitis, leading to falsely reassuring initial CSF cell counts
Negative initial Gram stain and cultures may reflect prior empiric antibiotic treatment (ceftriaxone given 2 months ago) or early disease stage

The oral ulcer and behavioral changes (disinhibition, inappropriate laughter) are red herrings that suggest neurosyphilis but are explained by aphthous ulceration and frontal lobe cerebritis respectively
Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in HIV-infected adults and can present indolently
The right frontal subcortical lesion on MRI represents early cerebritis, a known complication of pneumococcal meningitis
Elevated opening pressure (28 cmH2O) with near-normal cell count should raise suspicion for partially treated infection or an atypical pathogen
The prior empiric treatment 2 months ago may have suppressed but not eradicated the infection, leading to this recurrent or persistent presentation
Repeat LP, blood cultures, and CSF PCR panel are essential when initial cultures are negative in a high-suspicion clinical scenario

metadata.condition_name: Acute bacterial meningitis (pneumococcal) in HIV-infected patient
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to atypical CSF with albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, near-normal cell count) in an immunocompromised host. Initial Gram stain and cultures are negative from prior antibiotic treatment. Red herrings include an oral ulcer suggesting neurosyphilis, behavioral changes mimicking HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder, and a right frontal lesion raising concern for PML. The critical diagnostic clue emerges only on follow-up testing (repeat LP, PCR panel, blood cultures).
